mnemonics.title: CEJ Sequence
mnemonics.content: Create-Execute-Join: Fundamental thread lifecycle stages in Pthreads (pthread_create → thread_function → pthread_join)

mnemonics.title: UK Thread Types
mnemonics.content: User-Kernel: ULTs (User-Level Threads) are managed in user space, KLTs (Kernel-Level Threads) require OS intervention. Remember 'UK' like United Kingdom of threading

mnemonics.title: Pthread Functions Chain
mnemonics.content: Cats Jump Everywhere: Create → Join → Exit. Order of common Pthread operations (pthread_create, pthread_join, pthread_exit)

mnemonics.title: Thread Arguments Mnemonic
mnemonics.content: I Am Functioning Arguments: Parameters order in pthread_create(&thread_ID, Attributes, Function, Args)

acronyms.usage: Helps remember the standard API for thread management in Unix/Linux systems
Differentiates between thread types based on management layer
Recall Java's historical user-level thread implementation

visualTips.visualization: Imagine spawning identical worker clones (threads) from a main character (process), all sharing the same toolbox (memory space)
Picture a conveyor belt (CPU) with multiple workers (threads) taking turns to place/remove items (tasks)
Visualize a shared whiteboard (process memory) that multiple team members (threads) can simultaneously write on
A book (process) with multiple sticky notes (threads). Destroying the book removes all notes, but removing a note leaves the book intact

analogies.analogy: Like waiters in a restaurant - multiple servers (threads) handle different tables (tasks) simultaneously, all under one restaurant (process)
Traffic lights at intersections - controls access to shared resources (road junctions) to prevent collisions (race conditions)
Office workflow - Many:Many model = multiple workers (user threads) assigned to multiple machines (kernel threads), like a task force with shared computers
